---
title: ""
---

<div class="section">
<p class="section-title">Dissertation Research</p>

## Post-Fire Rain-on-Snow Hydrology in the Eastern Cascades

Wildfire fundamentally alters the forest canopy that regulates snowpack energy balance. 
In the Eastern Cascades, where rain-on-snow events drive some of the most dangerous 
flood and debris flow hazards, understanding how canopy loss changes snowmelt timing 
and magnitude is an urgent and open question.

My dissertation uses remote sensing, field instrumentation, and long-term streamflow 
records to quantify these effects across a gradient of burn severity at two fire sites 
in Washington State.

---

### Field Sites

| Site | Fire | Year | Watershed |
|------|------|------|-----------|
| Labor Mountain | Labor Mountain Fire | 2023 | Tillicum Creek |
| Lower Sugarloaf | Lower Sugarloaf Fire | 2024 | Peshastin Creek |
| Squilchuck Creek | — | — | Unburned control |

---

### Dissertation Papers

| | Topic | Key Data | Status |
|-|-------|----------|--------|
| **Paper 1** | Does RAVG canopy loss predict spatial snowpack depletion during the December 2025 atmospheric river? | MODIS fSCA · Sentinel-1 SAR · Landsat dNBR · RAVG CC-5 | In progress |
| **Paper 2** | Regional rain-on-snow detection across the western US | SNOTEL · MODIS · NWM | Planned |
| **Paper 3** | Historical burned vs. unburned streamflow response — Eastern Cascades | USGS 15-min streamflow · MTBS 2009–2024 | Planned |
| **Paper 4** | Field instrumentation and monitoring network design | Timelapse cameras · snow stakes · tipping buckets · pressure transducers | Planned |

---

### Remote Sensing Tools

- **MODIS fSCA** — fractional snow cover area time series
- **Sentinel-1 SAR** — wet snow detection
- **Landsat dNBR** — burn severity mapping
- **RAVG CC-5** — canopy cover change post-fire
- **Google Earth Engine** — cloud-based processing platform

---

### Monitoring Network

Field instrumentation deployed at Labor Mountain and Lower Sugarloaf includes 
timelapse cameras, snow stakes, tipping bucket precipitation gauges, pressure 
transducers, and temperature/humidity loggers. Reference SNOTEL stations: 
Pope Ridge, Harts Pass, Rainy Pass, and Blewett Pass.

</div>

<div class="section">
<p class="section-title">Federal Research Context</p>

This work is embedded in my role as BAER (Burned Area Emergency Response) Coordinator 
at the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, where post-fire watershed assessment directly 
informs land management decisions and public safety. My dissertation bridges the gap 
between operational emergency response and long-term hydrologic research.

Tribal partnerships with the Yakama Nation and Colville Confederated Tribes inform 
field site access and research priorities.
</div>
